title: Gi Annoyed Grunt

jokes.text: we are not all naked under our clothes
jokes.setup: Chalkboard gag - Bart writing lines as punishment
jokes.punchline: Absurd logical statement that's technically true but pointless
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons absurdist humor - takes a common phrase and makes it meaninglessly literal. Good economy and originality.

jokes.text: Bart, can we go to Banana Republic? There's a mannequin there I have a crush on.
jokes.punchline: Reveals pathetically he has romantic feelings for an inanimate object
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse characterization - his hopeless romanticism taken to absurd extreme. Well-structured setup and payoff.

jokes.text: Milhouse, that is the most pathetic thing I ever... Oh, my God. She's beautiful.
jokes.setup: Bart mocking Milhouse's attraction to mannequin
jokes.punchline: Immediately becomes equally attracted himself mid-insult
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection joke with perfect timing. Shows Bart's hypocrisy and susceptibility to same weaknesses he mocks.

jokes.text: Bullies! Wait. They're employees. They have to be nice to us. Let's go.
jokes.punchline: Realizes retail workers are contractually obligated to be pleasant
jokes.explanation: Smart observational humor about retail dynamics. Shows Bart's cunning ability to exploit social situations.

jokes.text: What size do I smell like?
jokes.setup: Being asked shoe size by employee
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical question combining sight and smell inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor playing with mixed senses. Somewhat random but fits Bart's mischievous nature.

jokes.text: Dude, don't lose it in the workplace. We already on thin ice with Mr. Friedman.
jokes.punchline: School bullies are now concerned about workplace professionalism
jokes.explanation: Excellent character development joke - bullies learning workplace dynamics. Great ironic contrast to their usual behavior.

jokes.text: The shoe size calibrator is for measuring feet and nothing else.
jokes.setup: Bart's inappropriate use of foot measuring device
jokes.punchline: Manager's overly serious correction about proper equipment use
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from overly formal response to juvenile misbehavior. Functional but not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: I want it overy-undery, not undery-overy. Re-lace them all.
jokes.punchline: Made-up technical terminology for simple shoe lacing
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay creating fake technical jargon. Shows Bart's ability to sound authoritative about nonsense.

jokes.text: I'm leaving this job to work at Jolly Tamale because the hours are better and my mom's really sick.
jokes.punchline: Bully gives unexpectedly mature, responsible reasons for leaving
jokes.explanation: Character development humor showing Nelson's unexpected maturity and family responsibility beneath his tough exterior.

jokes.text: When you leave, what happens to your rule about not whaling on customers? That rule leaves with me.
jokes.punchline: The only thing preventing customer violence was one person's restraint
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy with perfect setup-punchline structure. Implies imminent customer violence in deadpan delivery.

jokes.text: Yo, I don't know what I dig more, hip-hop, krunk, or serving my country.
jokes.setup: Army recruiter trying to appeal to youth
jokes.punchline: Awkward attempt to sound hip while recruiting
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing military's awkward youth outreach. Captures the disconnect between institutions and youth culture.

jokes.text: What? Being gay? Close. The Army.
jokes.setup: Bart asking if recruiters are hitting on them
jokes.punchline: Recruiter's uncomfortable comparison between gay recruitment and military recruitment
jokes.explanation: Sharp, edgy humor playing on military recruitment anxiety and social tensions. Great economy and timing.

jokes.text: Not wars, global struggles. You guys like globes?
jokes.punchline: Desperately weak attempt to make 'global' sound appealing by referencing globes
jokes.explanation: Good satire of military euphemisms combined with recruiter's desperate attempt at connection. Shows institutional dishonesty.

jokes.text: Even the dumbest teenagers in the dumbest town in the dumbest state know better than to join the Army.
jokes.setup: Recruiter's frustration with lack of recruits
jokes.punchline: Escalating insults about Springfield's intelligence while admitting recruitment failure
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with self-referential humor about Springfield's reputation. Nice comedic rhythm.

jokes.text: What's this assembly for? A surprise Doodlebops concert?
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent hope for children's entertainment at school
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph characterization - childlike innocence and misunderstanding of situations. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Well, what if there was a violent video game that you could play for free, plus it's real life and not a game at all?
jokes.setup: Recruiter asking about video game interest
jokes.punchline: Describing actual warfare as a free video game
jokes.explanation: Dark satirical humor about military recruitment tactics and violence normalization. Well-crafted ironic structure.

jokes.text: Do you guys know where we can find some awesome kids to show it to? Dearborn, Michigan? No, here.
jokes.setup: Looking for kids to show recruitment film to
jokes.punchline: Random geographic non-sequitur followed by obvious correction
jokes.explanation: Weak humor relying on random geography reference. Feels somewhat forced and doesn't land strongly.

jokes.text: I'm imagining that knight is me
jokes.punchline: Bart projecting himself into military fantasy imagery
jokes.explanation: Simple character moment showing Bart's susceptibility to military propaganda. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Now you can't legally join the army until you're 18, but if you pre-enlist now, we will save you a spot in America's next unresolvable conflict.
jokes.punchline: Euphemistically promising future wars will be unwinnable
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark political satire with perfect euphemistic language. Sharp commentary on military conflicts wrapped in recruitment speak.

jokes.text: Thanks for fitting us in, Sergeant Skinner. Well, I'd do anything for my beloved Army. Well, how about re-enlisting? How about you bite me?
jokes.setup: Skinner expressing love for the Army
jokes.punchline: Immediate hostile rejection when asked to actually rejoin
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-punchline showing gap between Skinner's nostalgia and reality. Great character consistency and timing.

jokes.text: Well, I'd do anything for my beloved Army. Well, how about re-enlisting? How about you bite me?
jokes.setup: Skinner expressing love for the Army
jokes.punchline: Immediately refusing to re-enlist when asked
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection showing Skinner's complex relationship with the military. Great structure and character consistency.

jokes.text: Eh, big deal, by the time Bart's 18, we're going to control the world. We're China, right?
jokes.setup: Homer dismissing concern about Bart joining army
jokes.punchline: Confused belief that America will become China
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic combining geopolitical confusion with characteristic optimism. Good character integration.

jokes.text: How come I can't join the army, but Lisa gets to be in PETA? I think she just answers their phone.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's activism is just phone duty
jokes.explanation: Solid family dynamic humor deflating Lisa's activism. Good setup-punchline structure with character-specific details.

jokes.text: Fur is murder! When's the Krusty movie coming out?
jokes.setup: Lisa delivering passionate animal rights message
jokes.punchline: Immediately switching to mundane entertainment inquiry
jokes.explanation: Good juxtaposition humor showing Lisa's ability to compartmentalize activism and normal kid interests. Nice economy.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm a real class act.
jokes.setup: Lisa participating in PETA demonstration involving disturbing imagery
jokes.punchline: Self-aware sarcastic comment about her activism methods
jokes.explanation: Character development humor showing Lisa's self-awareness about her extreme activism methods. Good ironic tone.

jokes.text: Sounds, uh, like you're kind of tired of being bossed around at home.
jokes.setup: Homer mentioning Marge made him come
jokes.punchline: Recruiter exploiting marital dynamics for recruitment
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy about predatory recruitment tactics exploiting personal vulnerabilities. Well-observed psychology.

jokes.text: Hey, a big target like you will be home a lot sooner than that.
jokes.setup: Homer worried about being away from family for two years
jokes.punchline: Recruiter implying Homer will die quickly due to his size
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect economy and misdirection. Brutal military recruitment honesty delivered as false comfort.

jokes.text: Where's my parade? We have this issue of Parade Magazine.
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation offering magazine instead
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay joke with good setup-punchline structure. Shows military's cheapness and Homer's naivety.

jokes.text: Army private: $14,000 a year. D'oh!
jokes.punchline: His characteristic exclamation upon realizing low pay
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer reaction joke with his signature catchphrase. Functional character moment but predictable.

jokes.text: Homer, with all the things you've done-- go into space, attend clown college, join the Navy-- I never thought you'd join the Army.
jokes.punchline: Treating space travel and clown college as equally valid experiences
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback humor that treats Homer's ridiculous past adventures matter-of-factly. Great series continuity.

jokes.text: Who else is gonna keep oil under a hundred dollars a barrel? You? Don't make me laugh.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous belief that he personally controls oil prices
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor showing Homer's inflated sense of importance and misunderstanding of global economics.

jokes.text: You couldn't... Aw, oh, oh, don't, no. Daddy's just teasing. Of course you could. You could keep it under a hundred. Yes, you could. No, she couldn't.
jokes.setup: Homer starting to insult Maggie's abilities
jokes.punchline: Flip-flopping between protecting baby's feelings and honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Homer's internal conflict between honesty and parental protectiveness. Nice escalation structure.

jokes.text: 'Cause mine is I care too much.
jokes.setup: Drill sergeant asking about major malfunctions
jokes.punchline: Recruit claiming caring too much is his flaw
jokes.explanation: Standard job interview cliche humor. Recognizable trope but not particularly original or memorable.

jokes.text: While you've been standing here, your hair's been cut, and your clothes have been replaced with army fatigues.
jokes.punchline: Impossible instantaneous transformation while talking
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist visual gag with cartoon logic. Shows military efficiency taken to impossible extreme. Strong visual comedy.

jokes.text: Here's your nicknames: Brooklyn, Hollywood, Kissimmee-St. Cloud, Florida, Maverick, Blanket Hog, Newman's Own, Master of Suspense, England's Rose, Nickname Pending, Bram Stoker's Dracula, and Snowflake.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous and inappropriate nicknames
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation with creative, unexpected nicknames. Great variety and building absurdity. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: What happens to me in the summer?
jokes.punchline: Practical concern about seasonal appropriateness
jokes.explanation: Smart observational humor showing Homer's literal thinking and practical concerns. Good character-specific logic.

jokes.text: You're gonna sit here and eat donuts, while the rest of the unit does push-ups! Just for that, they'll do the push-ups one-handed, while you eat Alaskan King salmon and I give you a foot rub!
jokes.punchline: Punishments become increasingly luxurious rewards
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation humor subverting military punishment expectations. Great structure building to absurd luxury.

jokes.text: I like rubbing people's feet, Corns and bunions are a treat, can give massage real well, If you know why, don't ask, don't tell.
jokes.punchline: Singing about foot fetish with military policy reference
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay combining foot fetish humor with military policy. Good character subversion of tough sergeant stereotype.

jokes.text: Can you say, 'Side effects may include drowsiness and loss of appetite'?
jokes.punchline: Requesting pharmaceutical commercial impression
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about distinctive voice-over work. Shows Homer's pop culture awareness and inappropriate timing.

jokes.text: Hmm, sounded better in my head
jokes.setup: Commander delivers the line Homer requested
jokes.punchline: Homer's disappointed reaction to the performance
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to previous joke with good character consistency. Shows Homer's unrealistic expectations meeting reality.

jokes.text: Frontline infantry. Frontline infantry. Frontline infantry. Frontline infantry. I joined to make recruitment films. Well, you know where you're gonna get good footage, don't you? Ooh, tell me. Frontline infantry!
jokes.punchline: Even aspiring filmmaker gets combat assignment for 'footage'
jokes.explanation: Good running gag with dark twist. Shows military's cynical use of soldiers' dreams for recruitment purposes.

jokes.text: That's an odd way to start handing out medals.
jokes.setup: Commander explaining their low test scores
jokes.punchline: Homer's confused expectation of reward for failure
jokes.explanation: Good character humor showing Homer's eternal optimism and misreading of situations. Nice ironic structure.

jokes.text: Don't you get it? They're using us as patsies to make the Army look good. Are we that stupid?
jokes.punchline: Homer's innocent question proving the point
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-punchline with Homer's question inadvertently confirming they are indeed that stupid. Great timing.

jokes.text: I'm gonna stay here and let them test their laser-guided super weapons on me. Like that one.
jokes.punchline: Casually pointing at weapon aimed at him
jokes.explanation: Good Homer obliviousness to danger. His casual attitude toward weapons testing fits his character.

jokes.text: Ah, stay crunchy in milk. I will! Stay crunchy, men. Stay crunchy.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to think like military leader
jokes.punchline: Adopting cereal slogan as military strategy
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor showing Homer's unique logic and leadership style. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: This'll be like shooting cats off of Grandma's sofa.
jokes.setup: Colonel expressing confidence about war games
jokes.punchline: Disturbing and nonsensical variation of common expression
jokes.explanation: Dark humor mixing animal cruelty with family imagery. Memorable but somewhat cheap shock value.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I just remembered. It's Chinese New Year! Gung hay fat choi! Gung hay fat choi!
jokes.setup: Homer in middle of military operation
jokes.punchline: Sudden cultural celebration interrupting warfare
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment showing Homer's scattered priorities and cultural awareness at inappropriate times.

jokes.text: I sure as hell can! Congress slipped it into the National Broccoli Day Proclamation.
jokes.setup: Being told he can't invade American city
jokes.punchline: Hidden military authorization in vegetable proclamation
jokes.explanation: Excellent political satire about hidden legislation in mundane bills. Original concept with great comedic specificity.

jokes.text: Whew, this pain feels so real. Uh, it is real. Oh. Guess I'd better do my part.
jokes.punchline: Casual acceptance of real danger after clarification
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Homer's casual attitude toward personal danger. Nice setup-punchline structure.

jokes.text: What you doing, Moe? Drawing a wang on Marmaduke? Heck, no. I'm challenging myself with one of these, uh, Sudoku games.
jokes.setup: Barney asking what Moe is doing
jokes.punchline: Assuming Moe is vandalizing comics
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor. Barney's assumption about Moe's activities fits both characters.

jokes.text: What, that Japanese puzzle in which no numeral can be repeated in a row, column, or box? That's how it works? I was just drawing wangs on the numbers.
jokes.punchline: Moe's juvenile misunderstanding of the puzzle
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy contrasting Barney's unexpected intelligence with Moe's crudeness. Well-structured reveal.

jokes.text: The Salvation Army? You got it. Not that Army, the one from Stripes!
jokes.setup: Homer asking for help hiding from Army
jokes.punchline: Moe's confusion between different 'armies' via movie reference
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay joke with pop culture reference. Good character-specific confusion from Moe.

jokes.text: Hey, some things mean more to me than money. Like a whole lot of money. Why did you just say that sentence fragment? Uh, it, uh--long story.
jokes.setup: Moe claiming some things matter more than money
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting himself, then awkward deflection
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Moe's greed overriding his attempts at nobility. Nice self-awareness moment.

jokes.text: How would you like to be stacked naked in a pile with a hillbilly girl pointing at you and laughing? That was our last Christmas card.
jokes.punchline: Revealing this was actually their family Christmas card
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy subverting expectations. Takes serious political reference and makes it absurdly domestic. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: How would you like to be stacked naked in a pile with a hillbilly girl pointing at you and laughing? That was our last Christmas card.
jokes.punchline: Revealing it was their Christmas card
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that takes serious subject and makes it absurd through the Christmas card revelation. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: 'H' means 'hot'?!
jokes.punchline: Shock at learning elementary plumbing knowledge
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer ignorance joke. Perfect character integration showing his obliviousness to basic knowledge.

jokes.text: I am not bald. I am balding! Ugh, why will no one honor the '-ding'? I honor the '-ding', sir. The hell are you talking about?
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy protesting his classification as bald
jokes.punchline: Obsessive attention to grammatical suffix, met with confusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy characterization with his pedantic nature. Great wordplay focusing on linguistic precision.

jokes.text: I don't belong here. I am not bald. I am balding! Ugh, why will no one honor the '-ding'?
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy protesting his detention
jokes.punchline: Pedantic distinction about balding vs. bald
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy pedantry. His obsession with grammatical precision in crisis is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Never back down. Never admit a mistake. That's why we've won over half the wars we've fought.
jokes.punchline: Admission of losing nearly half of all wars as success metric
jokes.explanation: Excellent political satire with perfect ironic structure. Sharp commentary on military spin and institutional arrogance.

jokes.text: Wow, uh... this'll be the first time I ever watered down my booze.
jokes.setup: Moe helping dump alcohol in water supply
jokes.punchline: Claiming this is his first time watering down drinks
jokes.explanation: Good ironic humor about Moe's business practices. The obvious lie fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: Wow, uh... this'll be the first time I ever watered down my booze. Moe, why are your eyes darting back and forth so much? Oh, I-I'm just thinking about a great tennis match I saw once, yeah.
jokes.setup: Moe claiming he's never watered down liquor
jokes.punchline: Nervous behavior revealing obvious lie about his business practices
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy revealing Moe's dishonest business practices through nervous physical comedy.

jokes.text: Uh, uh, now I'm thinking about ping-pong. A... ver-very fast chess game. A... snappy David Mamet play.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly sophisticated cultural references while obviously lying
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Moe's pretensions and desperation. Good callback structure with increasing absurdity.

jokes.text: Great Cheney's Ghost! You win.
jokes.punchline: Political figure reference as exclamation of defeat
jokes.explanation: Clever political reference as expletive substitute. Good economy and topical humor for its time.

tmdb_overview: Homer is bamboozled by Army recruiters and winds up in basic training, where he runs afoul of a tough-minded colonel and is assigned to play the enemy in war games.
